title: Why This Energy Shock Is Different
gist: The 2026 Iran-Gulf conflict is destroying oil supply rather than merely rerouting it, which breaks the standard policy toolkit; yet Brent at ~$79 sits far below April's $120 peak, signaling the market prices this as a violent renegotiation of Hormuz passage rather than an outright blockade.

insights.claim: Today's geopolitical energy shock is destroying supply rather than rerouting it, which exposes the limits of the standard macro policy toolkit designed for demand or price shocks.
insights.speaker: GENE FRIEDA
insights.so_what: A supply-destruction shock cannot be smoothed by rate cuts alone; watch for coordinated fiscal-monetary responses rather than single-lever central bank action.
insights.quote: today's geopolitical shock is destroying supply rather than rerouting it, exposing the limits of the standard policy toolkit

insights.claim: Governments can only cushion the energy shock's impact on the most exposed households and firms if fiscal and monetary policy are used in concert rather than in isolation.
insights.speaker: GENE FRIEDA
insights.so_what: Policy coordination becomes the key variable; watch for targeted fiscal transfers paired with monetary restraint rather than blanket subsidies.

insights.claim: The Gulf ceasefire in mid-2026 collapsed after barely three weeks, showing the fragility of any de-escalation in the Iran conflict.
insights.speaker: GENE FRIEDA
insights.so_what: Short-lived ceasefires argue against pricing durable calm into energy or risk assets.
insights.quote: The Gulf ceasefire lasted barely three weeks.

insights.claim: After Iranian attacks on three commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz, the US struck more than 80 targets, revoked Iran's oil-sanctions waiver, and declared the memorandum of understanding over.
insights.speaker: GENE FRIEDA
insights.so_what: Revoking the sanctions waiver removes Iranian barrels from legal supply; watch for the barrel volume affected and enforcement intensity.

insights.claim: Brent crude rose to roughly $79 per barrel on the renewed war, a meaningful jump but far below April's $120 peak, when the Strait of Hormuz was closed outright.
insights.speaker: GENE FRIEDA
insights.so_what: The ~$79 vs $120 gap gives a market-implied ceiling on the blockade scenario; a move back toward $120 would signal the market is repricing outright closure.
insights.quote: Brent crude rose to around $79 per barrel—a meaningful jump, but far below April's $120 peak, when the strait was closed outright.
insights.locator: Yet the market response was telling

insights.claim: The gap between renewed open war and restrained oil prices suggests the market is pricing a violent renegotiation of the terms of passage through Hormuz rather than a return to full blockade.
insights.speaker: GENE FRIEDA
insights.so_what: Frames the binary trade: hedge for blockade tail risk while base-casing a negotiated, tolled passage regime.
insights.quote: Is this the road back to blockade, or a violent renegotiation of the terms of passage?
insights.locator: That gap between renewed war and restrained prices
